texts.label: text
texts.orig: On a bright summer afternoon, Aavi and Thirav enjoy their shikanji lemonade). After looking at the ice in the lemonade, Thirav wonders about the nature of ice and water.
texts.text: On a bright summer afternoon, Aavi and Thirav enjoy their shikanji lemonade). After looking at the ice in the lemonade, Thirav wonders about the nature of ice and water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can put water in the freezer of a refrigerator and check if it gets converted into ice.
texts.text: We can put water in the freezer of a refrigerator and check if it gets converted into ice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yes, I know when water is left in the freezer, it gets converted into ice but probably something gets added to the ice in the freezer.
texts.text: Yes, I know when water is left in the freezer, it gets converted into ice but probably something gets added to the ice in the freezer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you think Thirav is right? How can you find out?
texts.text: Do you think Thirav is right? How can you find out?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.1: Let us observe
texts.text: Activity 8.1: Let us observe

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Putanice cube ina cup, leave it on the table and observe.
texts.text: ¢ Putanice cube ina cup, leave it on the table and observe.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ice gets converted into water.
texts.text: Ice gets converted into water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What can you conclude from the observations?
texts.text: What can you conclude from the observations?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Does this mean that ice and water are the same substance? Yes, ice and water are the two forms of the same substance. These forms are also called states. These different states of water show many differences in their behaviour. Water flows but ice does not. Water splashes but ice does not.
texts.text: Does this mean that ice and water are the same substance? Yes, ice and water are the two forms of the same substance. These forms are also called states. These different states of water show many differences in their behaviour. Water flows but ice does not. Water splashes but ice does not.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8.) [Investigating Water's Disappearing Act
texts.text: 8.) [Investigating Water's Disappearing Act

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is a rainy morning. While going to school, Aavi and Thirav observe that there are a lot of water puddles in the playground. That evening, when they go to play, they are surprised to observe that some of the water in the puddles had disappeared.
texts.text: It is a rainy morning. While going to school, Aavi and Thirav observe that there are a lot of water puddles in the playground. That evening, when they go to play, they are surprised to observe that some of the water in the puddles had disappeared.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I think the water has been absorbed by the soil on the playground. What do you, think about it?
texts.text: I think the water has been absorbed by the soil on the playground. What do you, think about it?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you ever noticed water in the puddles disappearing? Where does it go? Discuss with your friends.
texts.text: Have you ever noticed water in the puddles disappearing? Where does it go? Discuss with your friends.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Where else have you seen water disappearing? Can you think of a possible reason why this happens?
texts.text: Where else have you seen water disappearing? Can you think of a possible reason why this happens?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might have observed that after washing the utensils, water left on the surface of the utensils, dries up after some time. Does the reason you thought earlier to explain water disappearance apply in this case also?
texts.text: You might have observed that after washing the utensils, water left on the surface of the utensils, dries up after some time. Does the reason you thought earlier to explain water disappearance apply in this case also?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aavi wonders if water has seeped through the surface of the utensils.
texts.text: Aavi wonders if water has seeped through the surface of the utensils.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thirav thinks that water does not seep through the surface of the utensils. Design an activity to investigate whose idea is correct.
texts.text: Thirav thinks that water does not seep through the surface of the utensils. Design an activity to investigate whose idea is correct.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.2: Let usinvestigate
texts.text: Activity 8.2: Let usinvestigate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take a tablespoon of water on a steel plate as shown in Fig. 8.1.
texts.text: ¢ Take a tablespoon of water on a steel plate as shown in Fig. 8.1.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Observe whether water seeps through to the other side of the plate or not.
texts.text: @ Observe whether water seeps through to the other side of the plate or not.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Keep observing this at regular intervals until the water completely disappears.
texts.text: @ Keep observing this at regular intervals until the water completely disappears.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What do you infer? Is this activity enough to come to the conclusion that water does not seep through a steel plate?
texts.text: What do you infer? Is this activity enough to come to the conclusion that water does not seep through a steel plate?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If water does not seep through the steel plate. Then, where has the water gone?
texts.text: If water does not seep through the steel plate. Then, where has the water gone?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This water gets converted into gaseous state called water vapour. The water vapour is another state of water. Let us think of another observation where you notice the water disappearing.
texts.text: This water gets converted into gaseous state called water vapour. The water vapour is another state of water. Let us think of another observation where you notice the water disappearing.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 8.1: Steel plate with a tablespoon of water
texts.text: Fig. 8.1: Steel plate with a tablespoon of water

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While making dosa, we sprinkle some water on the hot pan and it disappears. Where does it go?
texts.text: While making dosa, we sprinkle some water on the hot pan and it disappears. Where does it go?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Let us draw
texts.text: Let us draw

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Draw a detailed sketch (with labels and caption) about what happens to the water.
texts.text: Draw a detailed sketch (with labels and caption) about what happens to the water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water which is sprinkled on the hot pan gets converted into steam. Steam is actually water vapour, some part of which converts into water droplets.
texts.text: a . The water which is sprinkled on the hot pan gets converted into steam. Steam is actually water vapour, some part of which converts into water droplets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation.
texts.text: The process of conversion of water into its vapour state is called evaporation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The process of evaporation takes place continuously, even at room temperature. Can you think of other examples of evaporation?
texts.text: The process of evaporation takes place continuously, even at room temperature. Can you think of other examples of evaporation?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Drying of wet clothes, mopped floor, and sweat on our body are some examples of it.
texts.text: Drying of wet clothes, mopped floor, and sweat on our body are some examples of it.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nowwhat do youthinkis the reason for the disappearance of water from the puddles? Is it due to: (i) seeping of water into the ground or (ii) evaporation of water or (iii) both of these?
texts.text: Now what do you thinkis the reason for the disappearance of water from the puddles? Is it due to: (i) seeping of water into the ground or (ii) evaporation of water or (iii) both of these?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hand sanitiser disappears as you rub it on your hands. What happens to it?
texts.text: Hand sanitiser disappears as you rub it on your hands. What happens to it?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: §.2 Another Mystery
texts.text: §.2 Another Mystery

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next day, Aavi, Thirav and their friends decide to make lemonade. During the preparation, they take cold water ina glass tumbler and add ice cubes into it. After a few minutes, they notice something exciting about the outer surface of the glass tumbler.
texts.text: Next day, Aavi, Thirav and their friends decide to make lemonade. During the preparation, they take cold water ina glass tumbler and add ice cubes into it. After a few minutes, they notice something exciting about the outer surface of the glass tumbler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us find out by conducting a similar activity ourselves.
texts.text: Let us find out by conducting a similar activity ourselves.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.3: Let us experiment
texts.text: Activity 8.3: Let us experiment

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take cold water in a glass tumbler.
texts.text: ¢ Take cold water in a glass tumbler.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Adda few ice cubes into it as shown in Fig. 8.2.
texts.text: @ Adda few ice cubes into it as shown in Fig. 8.2.

texts.text: Fig. 8.2: A glass tumbler containing cold water and ice cubes

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Leave it undisturbed for five minutes and observe it.
texts.text: ¢ Leave it undisturbed for five minutes and observe it.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Record your observations and the questions that arise in your mind in Table 8.1. You can also touch the outer surface of the glass tumbler to feel if there is any change.
texts.text: ¢ Record your observations and the questions that arise in your mind in Table 8.1. You can also touch the outer surface of the glass tumbler to feel if there is any change.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have many observations and questions here.
texts.text: You may have many observations and questions here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 8.1: Record the observations and questions
texts.text: Table 8.1: Record the observations and questions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One observation that arises in Aavi's mind is, "There are some water droplets (tiny drops) appearing on the outer surface of the glass tumbler." Initially, water droplets are deposited and these droplets combine together to form bigger drops. You can also try the above process with a metal container. You may be curious about where the water droplets come from.
texts.text: One observation that arises in Aavi's mind is, "There are some water droplets (tiny drops) appearing on the outer surface of the glass tumbler." Initially, water droplets are deposited and these droplets combine together to form bigger drops. You can also try the above process with a metal container. You may be curious about where the water droplets come from.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Suggest possible reasons explaining the appearance of water droplets on the outer surface of the glass tumbler.
texts.text: Suggest possible reasons explaining the appearance of water droplets on the outer surface of the glass tumbler.

texts.text: Discuss with your friends. Write down the possible reasons in Fig. 8.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have various possible reasons. You may agree or disagree with the reasons of others. Aavi and Thirav argued with a chain of reasons. What do you think about the possible reasons mentioned in Fig. 8.4?
texts.text: You may have various possible reasons. You may agree or disagree with the reasons of others. Aavi and Thirav argued with a chain of reasons. What do you think about the possible reasons mentioned in Fig. 8.4?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: I think that some of the water may have seeped out of the glass tumbler.
texts.text: I think that some of the water may have seeped out of the glass tumbler.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It might have decreased, but may not be significant enough to be seen.
texts.text: It might have decreased, but may not be significant enough to be seen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No, it cannot seep out. The level of water in the glass tumbler has not decreased.
texts.text: No, it cannot seep out. The level of water in the glass tumbler has not decreased.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With a tall and narrow bottle, even a slight change in the level of water is noticeable.
texts.text: With a tall and narrow bottle, even a slight change in the level of water is noticeable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We can take water at room temperature in another tumbler and find out whether any water seeps out.
texts.text: We can take water at room temperature in another tumbler and find out whether any water seeps out.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 8.4: Chain of reasoning
texts.text: Fig. 8.4: Chain of reasoning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continue the discussion on the given reasons or conduct activities to find evidence for the reasons given to help in this discussion. Where else have you seen water droplets like this?
texts.text: Continue the discussion on the given reasons or conduct activities to find evidence for the reasons given to help in this discussion. Where else have you seen water droplets like this?

texts.orig: Dew drops on plants
texts.text: Dew drops on plants

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might have seen dew drops on plants. Why do we see dew drops more in the morning? When we boil the water in a half-filled utensil and cover it with a steel plate, some water drops accumulate on the inner side of the steel plate. Where do these water drops come from? What do you think?
texts.text: You might have seen dew drops on plants. Why do we see dew drops more in the morning? When we boil the water in a half-filled utensil and cover it with a steel plate, some water drops accumulate on the inner side of the steel plate. Where do these water drops come from? What do you think?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When the water vapour present in the air comes in contact with a cold surface, it forms water droplets. The process of conversion of water vapour into its liquid state is called condensation.
texts.text: When the water vapour present in the air comes in contact with a cold surface,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After understanding the concept of condensation of water, let us go back to Activity 8.3. Could the water appearing on the outer surface of the glass tumbler in Activity 8.3 also be due to condensation of water vapour present in the air? Let us investigate it.
texts.text: After understanding the concept of condensation of water, let us go back to Activity 8.3. Could the water appearing on the outer surface of the glass tumbler in Activity 8.3 also be due to condensation of water vapour present in the air? Let us investigate it.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.4: Let us measure
texts.text: Activity 8.4: Let us measure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aavi and Thirav conduct an activity to find evidence for their reasons. You can also conduct the activity by following the steps given below. Record your data in Table 8.2.
texts.text: Aavi and Thirav conduct an activity to find evidence for their reasons. You can also conduct the activity by following the steps given below. Record your data in Table 8.2.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take a glass tumbler half-filled with water containing a few ice cubes. Cover it with a small steel plate. Weigh it on a digital weighing balance.
texts.text: ¢ Take a glass tumbler half-filled with water containing a few ice cubes. Cover it with a small steel plate. Weigh it on a digital weighing balance.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Observe the reading on the balance and record the weight after every five minutes.
texts.text: @ Observe the reading on the balance and record the weight after every five minutes.

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: ¢ Continue observing for 30 minutes. Record your observations in Table 8.2.

texts.text: Predict what will happen to the mass of cold water kept on the digital weighing balance. Will it increase or decrease or remain the same?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do your findings match with your predictions? Explain your observations.
texts.text: Do your findings match with your predictions? Explain your observations.

texts.text: You may observe some water droplets on the glass tumbler. Water vapour from the air comes in contact with the cold and gets converted into water droplets on the glass tumbler surface of the glass tumbler

texts.label: text
texts.orig: through condensation. There is an increase in the reading on the digital weighing balance. Can we conclude that water is not seeping through the wall of the tumbler? Can we also conclude that the water collected outside the tumbler is only due to condensation? No, we cannot say that conclusively from Activity 8.4. What more can you do to show that water is not seeping from the glass tumbler? How would you modify Activity 8.4 to find the answer?

Can we also conclude that the water collected outside the tumbler is only due to condensation? No, we cannot say that conclusively from Activity 8.4. What more can you do to show that water is not seeping from the glass tumbler? How would you modify Activity 8.4 to find the answer?

texts.orig: Repeat Activity 8.4 with the following modifications—
texts.text: Repeat Activity 8.4 with the following modifications—

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Mark the water level on the glass tumbler with a permanent marker or a visible tape.
texts.text: @ Mark the water level on the glass tumbler with a permanent marker or a visible tape.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What do you observe? Water level in the glass tumbler does not go down but the extra water gets collected on the outer surface of the glass tumbler. What can you conclude
texts.text: What do you observe? Water level in the glass tumbler does not go down but the extra water gets collected on the outer surface of the glass tumbler. What can you conclude

texts.label: text
texts.orig: from this? This activity shows that water is not seeping from the glass tumbler and the extra water is getting collected because of condensation.
texts.text: from this? This activity shows that water is not seeping from the glass tumbler and the extra water is getting collected because of condensation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8.3 What are the Different States of Water?
texts.text: 8.3 What are the Different States of Water?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water is a substance that can be observed in three different states in our daily life. In the solid state, it exists as ice. On heating, the ice melts and gets converted into its liquid state. On further heating, water gets converted into its gaseous state. Let us perform Activity 8.5 to identify the properties of different states of water.
texts.text: Water is a substance that can be observed in three different states in our daily life. In the solid state, it exists as ice. On heating, the ice melts and gets converted into its liquid state. On further heating, water gets converted into its gaseous state. Let us perform Activity 8.5 to identify the properties of different states of water.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.5: Let us identify
texts.text: Activity 8.5: Let us identify

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Put an ice cube in one container and transfer it to another container of different shape. What changes do you notice in the shape of the ice cube? Record your observations in Table 8.3.
texts.text: ¢ Put an ice cube in one container and transfer it to another container of different shape. What changes do you notice in the shape of the ice cube? Record your observations in Table 8.3.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Pour water from one container to another container ofa different shape. Observe how water behaves compared to the ice cube and make a record. Did you notice how water flows from one container to the other? What happens to its shape?
texts.text: ¢ Pour water from one container to another container ofa different shape. Observe how water behaves compared to the ice cube and make a record. Did you notice how water flows from one container to the other? What happens to its shape?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Pour water on a clean surface and observe how it spreads.
texts.text: ¢ Pour water on a clean surface and observe how it spreads.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ When water gets converted into water vapour, how does this water vapour spread? Compare this with the spreading behaviour of water.
texts.text: @ When water gets converted into water vapour, how does this water vapour spread? Compare this with the spreading behaviour of water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the differences in the properties of water in solid, liquid and gaseous states?
texts.text: What are the differences in the properties of water in solid, liquid and gaseous states?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ice (solid state) retains its shape irrespective of the container in which it is placed while water takes the shape of the container. Ice does not flow or spread.
texts.text: Ice (solid state) retains its shape irrespective of the container in which it is placed while water takes the shape of the container. Ice does not flow or spread.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water (liquid state) flows and changes its shape. Water does not have a fixed shape. It takes up the shape of the container in which itis kept, but the volume of water remains constant. Does water also possess the property to spread? Yes, water also has the property to spread while keeping the volume constant.
texts.text: Water (liquid state) flows and changes its shape. Water does not have a fixed shape. It takes up the shape of the container in which itis kept, but the volume of water remains constant. Does water also possess the property to spread? Yes, water also has the property to spread while keeping the volume constant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water vapour (gaseous state) exhibits a property of spreading out in the entire available space. Gases do not possess a fixed shape. Water vapour exists even at room temperature; though it is invisible to us. It is present in the air around us. The water that evaporates during processes like drying of clothes or mopping of floors contribute to the water vapour in the air around us.
texts.text: Water vapour (gaseous state) exhibits a property of spreading out in the entire available space. Gases do not possess a fixed shape. Water vapour exists even at room temperature; though it is invisible to us. It is present in the air around us. The water that evaporates during processes like drying of clothes or mopping of floors contribute to the water vapour in the air around us.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You are now familiar with the three states of water. Some other substances also exhibit these states. For example, wax, oil and ghee. Let us look at some more examples of solids, liquids and gases.
texts.text: You are now familiar with the three states of water. Some other substances also exhibit these states. For example, wax, oil and ghee. Let us look at some more examples of solids, liquids and gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Look around and find some examples of solid substances. Some examples could be stones, wood and glass.
texts.text: Look around and find some examples of solid substances. Some examples could be stones, wood and glass.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the other examples of liquids you can think of? Here are two examples—milk and oil. Think of five more examples.
texts.text: What are the other examples of liquids you can think of? Here are two examples—milk and oil. Think of five more examples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Have you ever noticed that you can smell the food being cooked even without entering the kitchen? How does this smell reach us?
texts.text: Have you ever noticed that you can smell the food being cooked even without entering the kitchen? How does this smell reach us?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is because the smell of yummy food from cooking spreads through the air and reaches our nostrils, even if we are not in the kitchen.
texts.text: It is because the smell of yummy food from cooking spreads through the air and reaches our nostrils, even if we are not in the kitchen.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What are the other examples of gases you can think of? What about oxygen and carbon dioxide?
texts.text: What are the other examples of gases you can think of? What about oxygen and carbon dioxide?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8.4 How can We Change the States of Water?
texts.text: 8.4 How can We Change the States of Water?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: So far we have learnt that water can exist in solid, liquid and gaseous states. How can you change the state of water?
texts.text: So far we have learnt that water can exist in solid, liquid and gaseous states. How can you change the state of water?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can you quickly change ice to its liquid state, water?
texts.text: How can you quickly change ice to its liquid state, water?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If we have to change ice into water, and water into water vapour, we have to supply heat to it. If we want to change water into ice, what should be done?
texts.text: If we have to change ice into water, and water into water vapour, we have to supply heat to it. If we want to change water into ice, what should be done?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It can be done by placing water in a cold environment, such as a freezer. Water freezes and is
texts.text: It can be done by placing water in a cold environment, such as a freezer. Water freezes and is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: converted into ice. If we take the ice out of the freezer, it melts and is converted into water.
texts.text: converted into ice. If we take the ice out of the freezer, it melts and is converted into water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Can you think of any other example, besides water, that can change from solid to liquid?
texts.text: Can you think of any other example, besides water, that can change from solid to liquid?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A candle, which is made of wax, is one such example.
texts.text: A candle, which is made of wax, is one such example.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can we turn candle wax into liquid state? How can we change the liquid wax back into solid state? We should cool the liquid wax to change it into a solid. What are the other liquids you have seen which get converted into a solid? Have you ever seen coconut oil getting converted into its solid state during the winter season?
texts.text: How can we turn candle wax into liquid state? How can we change the liquid wax back into solid state? We should cool the liquid wax to change it into a solid. What are the other liquids you have seen which get converted into a solid? Have you ever seen coconut oil getting converted into its solid state during the winter season?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, we can see that water and other substances change their states on heating or cooling. The process of conversion of a solid into liquid state is called melting. The process of conversion of liquid into solid state is called freezing.
texts.text: Hence, we can see that water and other substances change their states on heating or cooling. The process of conversion of a solid into liquid state is called melting. The process of conversion of liquid into solid state is called freezing.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us check the connection between different states of water through Activity 8.6.
texts.text: Let us check the connection between different states of water through Activity 8.6.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 8.6: Let us complete the diagram
texts.text: Activity 8.6: Let us complete the diagram

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: §8§.5 How can Water be Evaporated Faster or Slower?
texts.text: §8§.5 How can Water be Evaporated Faster or Slower?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In section 8.1, we have learnt about evaporation. Let us explore it more!
texts.text: In section 8.1, we have learnt about evaporation. Let us explore it more!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observe your surroundings. What are the conditions that affect how fast water evaporates? What differences do you see in evaporation on a cold day versus a hot day? Discuss with your friends. The following words may help in your discussion—fan, drying cloth, sweating, windy day, hot day, rainy day.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aavi's mother purchased a new matka (earthen pot) to replace the stainless steel pot for storing drinking water. Upon returning from school, Aavi notices the earthen pot and drinks water from it. Aavi expresses surprise and asks, "Why is the water in the earthen pot so cold? I never observed water getting cold in a stainless steel pot." What do you think is the reason?
texts.text: Aavi's mother purchased a new matka (earthen pot) to replace the stainless steel pot for storing drinking water. Upon returning from school, Aavi notices the earthen pot and drinks water from it. Aavi expresses surprise and asks, "Why is the water in the earthen pot so cold? I never observed water getting cold in a stainless steel pot." What do you think is the reason?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8.7 How Do Clouds Give Us Rain?
texts.text: 8.7 How Do Clouds Give Us Rain?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Condensation plays a significant role in the process of bringing evaporated water back to the Earth's surface. How does this happen? When air moves higher above the Earth's surface, it becomes cooler and cooler. At certain heights, the air gets so cool that the water vapour in it turns into droplets which are generally formed
texts.text: Condensation plays a significant role in the process of bringing evaporated water back to the Earth's surface. How does this happen? When air moves higher above the Earth's surface, it becomes cooler and cooler. At certain heights, the air gets so cool that the water vapour in it turns into droplets which are generally formed

texts.label: text
texts.orig: around dust particles. These small droplets float in the air and form clouds. Many droplets join together to form bigger drops of water. Some drops get so heavy that they start falling. These falling water drops are what we call rain.
texts.text: around dust particles. These small droplets float in the air and form clouds. Many droplets join together to form bigger drops of water. Some drops get so heavy that they start falling. These falling water drops are what we call rain.

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water from the ocean and the Earth's surface evaporates into the atmosphere as vapour and returns as rain, hail or snow, ultimately flowing back to the oceans. This circulation of water is known as the water cycle.
texts.text: The water from the ocean and the Earth's surface evaporates into the atmosphere as vapour and returns as rain, hail or snow, ultimately flowing back to the _ oceans. This circulation of water is known as the water cycle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What did I do well? Was I able to label all "Parts of the water 'Y"I@? Which parts of "Water cycle were enc
texts.text: What did I do well? Was I able to label all 'Ne Parts of the water cycle? Which parts of '""® Water cycle were enc

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Only a small portion of water available on the Earth is fit for use by plants, animals and humans. Most of the water is in the oceans and it cannot be used directly. We use water for drinking and also for many other activities. The number of people using water is increasing with a rise in population. The increasing demand for water causes its shortage in many parts of the world. Hence, it is very important to use water wisely and avoid wasting it. Let us keep our water bodies free from pollution. You will learn more about water and its conservation in the chapter 'Nature's Treasures'.
texts.text: Only a small portion of water available on the Earth is fit for use by plants, animals and humans. Most of the water is in the oceans and it cannot be used directly. We use water for drinking and also for many other activities. The number of people using water is increasing with a rise in population. The increasing demand for water causes its shortage in many parts of the world. Hence, it is very important to use water wisely and avoid wasting it. Let us keep our water bodies free from pollution. You will learn more about water and its conservation in the chapter 'Nature's Treasures'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Allow 4-5 hours for the mini pot-in-pot cooler to cool down. The time range can be influenced by many conditions. Observe and discuss how it creates a cooling effect inside the pots. Keep some vegetables and fruits in it and observe for a week on a daily basis to check for the freshness of the vegetables and fruits kept inside the cooler. You will have to add water regularly to keep the sand moist. For how many days can the vegetables and fruits be kept fresh in it? What are the conditions which can affect the number of these days? What else can be used in place of sand for better cooling?
